speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Dennis Urstein, and this is tape number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I forgot to mention something. While I was in Belgium, uh, I knew that my sister was in England and that the-- the-- the-- that the real peculiar part was that I remembered the address where she lived. Today I haven't got a clue. I remembered everything. So I got a hold of a soldier, a British soldier, in Belgium in Brussels, who came from Vienna originally, lived in England and served in the army, and I gave him a letter. It was a six or eight-page letter where I wrote down everything that happened to me from the time of day one. I told her that my-- our parents got killed and when they got killed. And she still has that letter today. [LAUGHS] And so she wrote back. And she was a domestic down there, like all refugees were. And she didn't have much. So she tried. She tried, and there was nothing that she could do. But I didn't-- then I wrote her back, and I said I don't need anything. I've got everything I want. I got clothes. I got food. I got lodgings. You don't have to worry about a thing anyhow. That was a great day for me because I did know that I had an uncle in Switzerland that also crossed the border from Austria in 1938, I think. But this is it. That was my only family. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you received a letter from her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, several. Back and forward. I used to have people going back and forward down there in Belgium. And then when I was in Salzburg, it was easier because Americans used to go to England on their vacations or furlough. And so it was easier, and we kept contact. And then I had an address in Salzburg. I had-- I got a house that belonged to an SS obersturmbannfuhrer that was hanged by the Partisans in Yugoslavia. His name was Piesta. And we just walked in. That means me. And we took the house. And that it-- that was it. And then we had some people that were lodgers. There were two American soldiers that got married to Hungarian girls. And they lived upstairs. I lived downstairs. And then that-- then after I got married, Claire and I, we lived there. And that's where my daughter was born, not in the house, but while we lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work for the counterintelligence?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1945, middle June, I guess, until 1948. And that's when we decided, when this was over-- I didn't want to live in Austria anymore. I want to get out. And it is obvious that the only way we could get in to England was as domestics. So I became a butler with a black tie and a tail coat. And Claire became the cook. And there was a baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, I was a cocky young man. I said I'm not going to be a bloody butler. What the hell am I going to be a butler for? You know, I had this yes sir, no sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was His Lordship, and the other one was who knows what. But they were very wonderful people. I just didn't appreciate it. I mean, and then I hated the cleaning silver. I had to serve. And there were all kind of things that happened down there, so it was a rough period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sponsored by a specific family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody. No, no, no, no, no, no. We, we-- my mother-in-law, Claire's mother, arranged for jobs. And then we got the papers. And we were only allowed to work-- I was only allowed to work as a butler, and she was only allowed to work as a cook in tandem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said that your feeling about vengeance changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, there are a lot of things that changed. First of all, I hated all the Austrians and all the Germans. I wanted them all dead. And one day I woke up. It was just before I got sick, before I had my nervous breakdown, maybe a couple of months. I'll come to this in a second. And I said, you're stupid. How can you say a thing like that-- 'all?'

speaker: interviewer
text: He said 'all,' meaning Hitler. And then all of a sudden I said what about all these people that were not Jewish, that were Gentiles from Austria and from Germany that you met in the camp? So how can you say 'all?' This changed something. But it didn't change me trying to find the ones that were guilty of crimes. But my attitude changed. And then it was also a matter of age. If I would have met-- and I did not. At later dates I did. But if I would have met somebody that was as old as I am, OK, and I didn't know that person, it was always in the back of my mind what did this guy do during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: But if I met somebody who was 10 years younger than me, I said, what the heck. He was a kid. He couldn't have done anything. Anyhow, but very briefly, so we stayed in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to keep-- you gave up this job and moved to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't give up this feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We're still in contact. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, the feeling about the vengeance because I'm just trying to pursue this.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, I did not. I haven't done yet. And it's 50 years later. So it's-- that-- nothing has changed since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reunion with your sister like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ugh, don't ask. Don't ask. It was-- it-- what I'm going to say about this? It's just a matter of talking. It's-- it's-- it was great. It was-- we cried, and we hugged. And, you know, England was on ration. I met her before 1948. She was married in 1947. And I went to the wedding from Salzburg. Claire was at home in Salzburg, and I went to the wedding. And-- [LAUGHS] She'll never forgive me for that. They tried to-- they, they, they, they thought I was starving. I was hungry. I didn't want to-- Well, I ate. I ate everything but the kitchen sink. I just kept on eating and getting bigger, and bigger, and bigger. And they had-- I slept in a room. They had an apartment. There was a separate room there for me. And then they said, look, if you're hungry during the night, there is a can, a pullman can-- a pullman can weighs about three kilos-- of Spam. That it-- Spam to me was the great-- I still eat Spam if I can get it. I get it in the States, and I bring it over here. Love it. If you want to that-- so I opened that can of Spam.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? That three kilo, I ate it that one night. And my sister was looking for it a few days later. She says, what happened to the Spam that I had up there in the cupboard? I said, I ate it. She never forgives me for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, back to England. After we were in England, I started gaining a lot of weight. Matter of fact, I went up to 225 or 230 pounds. I looked like a balloon.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to hear what I ate? OK, I ate, for instance, I had-- I opened a can of Danish, Danish bacon. It was round, wound around papers. And I put Hershey chocolate sauce on there and ate that. During the night when we were married, Claire caught me one day, and she said, you're crazy. I said, yes, I know. But I'm hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, and I got bigger and bigger and bigger. And then all of a sudden I started dreaming about the camp. Never dreamt about it. If somebody asked me something, something put me up, this and that, and then I lost-- start losing weight. I lost 40 or 50 pounds again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother-in-law took me to a doctor, who was also a refugee. He came from Czechoslovakia. And he took one look at me, and he said something to my mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, we have to send him to a clinic. Nothing. So I went there with my mother-in-law. I had to have somebody go with me. We lived in a place called Cheltenham in England at that time. It was just a spa. And we were sent by bus, we went by bus to Gloucester, which is the capital of the province or whatever. And I see this big building down there, huge building, square, very dark, dark gray. And I walk in there. And we go to the receptionist. And we talk to them. And she sends us somewhere. We go downstairs. We go through a long corridor. And there were windows that were very, very high, so I jumped up to see what happened behind that window. And all of sudden I see people that had some handicaps jumping around, tongues hanging out, in the yard down there. And I said, oh my god, this is a mental home. And I kept on going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we sat down in a room. And the nurses were nuns with the habits. And there were about six or seven people with me in that same room down there. And she came in and told us to take off our belts, to take off-- to take out-- if we had any false teeth, to take it out, and this and that, but still keep on being dressed. That's all. Just keep your pants and your shirt on. And then they led a person at the time to a room on the other side of the corridor. OK? And then we heard this thumping noise, like somebody hit something, you know. Like you take a piece of wood and you hit the floor. No notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it was my turn. And I walk in there. There were two or three of nurses and a doctor with a white coat. Please lay down. I lay down. And they fastened something on my ankles. They fastened something on my temples. And then she says, please open your mouth, sir. And I get a piece of rubber pushed into my mouth. She says, bite. And the next thing I know, it was-- I woke up next day at home. What happened was I had shock treatment. And I went back down there, I think, about five or six times. Shock treatment to me was one that gave me a tremendous headache the next day. I thought my head was coming off. They sent me home by ambulance, not by bus, with my mother-in-law. That's why somebody had to come with me. That's what I found out. And then something happened that-- this is again, it's much, much later than that. We went to a swimming pool. And maybe it was 1950, OK, or '49. Because this happened in, I think '49 I think that happened. So it must have been '50. And we're sitting there, my wife, my daughter, and some friends in a public swimming pool. And all of a sudden I see two guys walking by, one very tall blond fellow. And I said to myself, I know that guy from somewhere. I know him. I know him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I am. I get up, and I go after him. He turns around, and he smiles at me. He says, you know me? I said yes. He has a very thick Czechoslovakian accent. In English I said yes, I know you, but I can't place you. Who are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, I can tell you the story right now. He said, I am a male nurse in Gloucester at the clinic that you were in, or the mental home, or whatever he called it, OK. And he said, when we dragged you out from that room after you had your shock treatment, we had to put you into a room on the other side on the floor. There were some mattresses or something. And you kept on screaming in German, I'm not going to go that way. This is the way to the crematorium. And he told me that they had to take me all around the building, downstairs. Inside. That's what calmed me down. And then they let me go. It's amazing. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you didn't give permission for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no. First of all, nobody asked you to give permission. And I'm very happy I got it. I got it again here in Canada. And I'm-- I'm-- you know that controversy that goes on about shock treatment? It helped me. It helped me, and it didn't help me. There are certain things that it helped me with, the important parts. The unimportant parts, I don't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I forgot things. I could talk about it. But then it hit me again, right here in 1956 or '57.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at the time, in the late '40s?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could talk about it, and it didn't bother me. And I gained weight again. I went back to my normal weight. And we stayed there. And then one day I said to Claire, I said honey, it's time to leave here. I don't want to be a butler, and I don't want you to be a cook.

speaker: interviewee
text: So her sister, Claire's sister, lived in Canada. She immigrated in the late or early '50s or something like that. No, in the early or late, late '40s, '48, '49, something like that. She lived here. And I wrote to her. OK, we made a plan. We took an atlas, and we said, OK, where are we going to go? Australia? It's too far. If I go to Australia, I'll  never see my sister again, OK. The United States? There was a reason that we didn't want to go to the United States because of a certain incident that happened, where I saw somebody-- where I heard the word, the N word as I call it, for the first time. And what the N's are doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, this guy is talking like the Nazis. He was a southerner. He was a redneck. And I said, if I go to the States, all I got to look for is trouble. Because if I hear that--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided on Canada by a finger. I just pointed to it. I said, that's where we're going to go. So I went to Canada House, showed them my papers, showed them my army papers, my discharge papers. And two days later they called me, and they said to me, they said, we got your visa, sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived in St. John on the 1st of February, 1951. And another beautiful thing happened. There are a few incidences that I love to talk about, and I like to remember. Is when the guy said welcome, the immigration officer. And I said hey, that's nice. No other significance, just the word welcome, OK. And so we travelled on the train from St. John, New Brunswick to the Union Station. My sister-in-law lived in a place called Wilcox Lake. I could have taken a bus, but I didn't know any better. And she didn't pick us up, so we were here after a long journey. It takes a long time to go by train from St. John to Toronto. And we were sitting there. It was, I think, 6 or 7 o'clock. We weren't hungry. We were seasick all the time, but we ate when we got off the boat. And we were sitting there, and all of sudden a fellow comes up again. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, are you Jewish? I said yes. I said, what the hell? What? Have I got it written on my forehead? He says, never mind. He said, I know. Did you just arrive? I said yes, that's my family. We just arrived. He again, he whips out a piece of paper, and he writes on there. He said-- there was-- I think the Jewish Congress at that time was on Beverley Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gives me the address. And he, just like a lawyer, he tells me what I can do and what I can't do. He said, if you need a loan, we will loan you some money.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you need anything, if you have-- I was very quite well dressed. He said, I don't think you need any,  any-- do you got any money on you? I said, yes. And he says, may I ask how much? I said yeah, $280. Oh, that's all right. That'll, that'll carry you through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, this beautiful feeling of belonging to a family. Now if this was the United Jewish Appeal, I don't know if it even existed at that time. But it was certainly the Congress.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know the name of the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have no idea. So, uh, all right, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to-- I want to just go over this a little bit quickly because we have lots of other things to talk about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so we go to Wilcox Lake. And the outhouse is outside. And it's freezing. It's about 22 below zero. And we are sitting down there. And my sister-in-law says hello and goodbye. And that's it. She lived next door in another cottage. It's-- but I don't carry any grudges. Let her live in peace. God bless her. She's a nut. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the first occupation you had here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the first one-- so I walked about-- it was about a mile and a half from the cottage to Yonge Street, to the highway. And I was holding my thumb up. And then somebody stopped, and I got in there. And I see this woman with blonde hair and a ponytail down there. And she looked like a typical German hausfrau. Now obviously, I have an accent. Some people say I haven't. I know that I have an accent, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started to talk. And she says, how long are you in Canada? I said we just arrived yesterday. Where you going? I said, I'm looking for a job. So she turns to her husband. And she says to me, are you German? I said no, I'm not from Germany. I didn't say German, not from Germany. I am from Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] She starts in German to me. And her husband is English. Her husband was a builder. She turns around, she says, give him a job. Just like that. I didn't know he was a builder. So he takes me out to Scarborough. He's building schools down there. And I carry in bricks. And it's cold. And it's wet. And I have a coverall, you know, the one that you step in with the zipper. It's not a two piece, it's a one piece thing that I brought from England. And I had an ambition to be a bricklayer because when I saw these guys laying bricks, I said what a wonderful job. They stay down there. They lay one brick. Then they smoke a cigarette and have a drink and lay another brick and they have a-- it was just marvelous, OK. I came home. And I had to walk back again. And it was cold. And when I arrived down there, I found Claire in bed. She was freezing to death. It was really cold. She makes supper. And I'm putting my coverall-- because it's   wet-- outside. The next morning I want to put it on. I can't put it on because it's stiff. It's frozen. It froze. So I stayed down there for, I think, about three days. And I said, that's not for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can just briefly go over your career after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I went to work in a restaurant. And I did very well because I pulled the chairs back when a lady sat down. Nobody did that. And my tips were very good. The only problem was that my tips didn't pay for my trip home if I would have taken a taxi, because the street car only went to a certain place. Then I had to take a taxi.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, I got away from this. And then I worked in a hotel that doesn't exist anymore, the Barkley Hotel, as a waiter. I mean I had experience as a butler, so I knew how to wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we moved back into a place on Hazelton Avenue, which is quite a hoi polloi area today, but at that time it was nothing. It was just a working district. And I went to look for a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought a newspaper. And there was an ad in there that they're looking for people in the car wash. And I said, go to a car wash. Where is it? This place called Spadina. So I asked where Spadina was. And I said, I'm going to arrive very early. So I go down there, and I'm 7 o'clock in the morning. And I see a guy running back and forth and screaming at people. And I said, I'm here for the job. And you know what he tells me? F-off. I said, I beg your pardon. He says, are you crazy? You should have been here at 5 o'clock in the morning. I said, it doesn't say so in the paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm devastated. I have no money. My wife had the money. I had maybe $1 in my pocket. And I walk around. And all of a sudden I see a picture window. There was a restaurant. And there's a big, huge picture on there. And it shows a blond, fat young man and Jimmy Durante. And I'm looking at this picture. And I'm starting to smile. And I start to chuckle. I actually laugh because I said, boy, Jimmy Durante with his nose up there like that and who--

speaker: interviewee
text: So a voice behind me says, why are you laughing? I said well, I know this is Jimmy Durante, but who is the fat guy? He said-- I turn around. Here is a man maybe 20 years my senior. And he said, that's my boss, Mr. Shopsowitz. I said, oh? He said, you know he knows a lot of-- and he starts talking to me. And then he says, would you like to have a coffee? I said yes. So we go in there, and we sit down. We have a coffee. And we talk. We talk about my background, about this, my education, and this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden about two hours later, Sam Shopsy, or Shopsowitz, or Shopsy as that-- Sam comes in. And this guy-- I forgot his name. He was the maitre d' in the restaurant. He run the restaurant. I forgot his name. I'm sorry. Early heimers, that's me. And Sam tells me about his ambitions. He wants to make a hot dog empire and corned beef, pastrami. He tells me about his brothers. He had two brothers, that one is in business with him. He's the younger one, Izzy. And then da, da, da, da. Da, da, da.

words.text: And then da, da, da, da.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, do you want a job? Do you want to be right in on the ground floor? Yes. I was the third employee.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a man down there who was the sales manager. I didn't know what he managed, what sales. His name was Eli Shotz. He's dead now. And me. And I became the Jew of all trades. I was the receiver, the shipper. And then we started the packaging department. I was interested in packaging. And I went to school at night. I went to Ryerson, and I took a packaging course down there. And I succeeded to get a certificate and all that kind of thing. And I worked down there. And there was one incident-- again, you know, it always comes back to you-- where I became-- I'm saying this in fun-- OK, I became a big shot down there. I was a boss.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is at Shopsy's.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Shopsy's, that's right. And one day I'm sitting at a toilet. And all of a sudden I hear two voices. And the one voice says, yeah, all these god damned Jews have all the money. We don't have nothing, but they got all the money. And I opened the door, and there's these two guys sitting down there who worked in a bank. This was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, give me an example. Who has all the money? He said, Taylor. I said, Taylor? Of the Argos Corporation. I said, but Taylor's not Jewish. He says, that's what you think. His name was probably Rubenstein. And I, you know, I said to myself, to me they're lowlifes. They are stupid. I mean, what am I going to talk about? I said, is the pope Jewish, too? Anyhow, there was just an incident.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had another incident that was at anti-Semitic. Right here I had a friend. When Claire was underground she was together with a girl, Emma, Emma Brown. And she lives here in Oshawa. And she got married to a young fella that was a prisoner of war, who told-- who knew her before the war started, OK. When the war started, he was conscripted in Vienna. He was gentile-- Charlie Brown. And he served in-- with Rommel. But before he left, he said to Emma-- he met her when she was underground. Da, da, da. He said, Emma, I can assure you about one thing. I'll be a prisoner of war as soon as I hit that place down there. And he was. And he spent his whole time in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I met my wife, she told me about her, about Emma and about her husband. He was a prisoner of war. And since I knew from Emma who he was, that he wasn't a Nazi. I got him out. And we became good friends. Our children are born within a month difference and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we were in Canada we corresponded. And they wanted to come over here. So I got him a job at General Motors. And one day we decided to go on a holiday to Muskoka. I think it was Gravenhurst. I'm not sure, but I think it was. I had my first car with a leaking bottom. So you just put a carpet over it and hope it doesn't rain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go. I make reservations from Shopsky's. I phoned them up, and I said, two couples that want to spend a weekend down there. One is Karl Brown because Karl was the German name for Charlie-- didn't change it-- and Dennis Urstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived down there, and we were all hot to trot. I go in there. And I say I'm Dennis Urstein, and I made reservations. So I want my keys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she looks at me, looks me up and down, and she says to me, I'm sorry. We haven't got a reservation for you. I said, oh my god, I phoned. I spoke to a gentleman here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walk outside. I'm really down. And Charlie could hardly speak English. He spoke English like you know, the [? 'sah-fet-tee' ?] shoes and all that kind of things. And all that. You know, he was an Austrian. He was Viennese.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I want to go in myself. I said, go in. So he comes in, he comes out with a key. That's when I smelled a rat. So I walked in there, and I really started screaming. And her husband came. And that's the guy I spoke to. I said-- well, I didn't use beautiful language, believe me. It wasn't political correct, but I really let him have it down there. So we all walked out, and we went to another place, and we got in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1953, I think. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work at Shopsy's?

speaker: interviewee
text: For 28 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I worked at Loblaws. And that was the best time of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your position at Loblaws?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was director of packaging. I loved it. I enjoyed it. I like to meet people. I'm, I'm firm. I'm nice. I don't sign contracts, I shake hands. And I still have friends now that I'm retired for a long time. But to go back again, then I got sick again. We went-- it was 1956 or 1957. We found some neighbors, the Balkans, he was a stockbroker. And we lived in a place on Bathurst and Finch called Mascot Place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day he said to me, he says, look, I have a friend who has a villa in Miami Beach. Why don't we go on a holidays? He had a very nice Thunderbird. And we got into the car, and we drove down there. We arrived, and the villa was on the island where Al Capone was. So when I was sitting down sunning myself and the excursion boats came by, I just waved my hand. And they thought I was a gangster. Who the hell knows? I had a ball down there. Anyhow, nice time. We came back. I got back into the office. We waited two weeks. And I had a friend who worked in the next office, Sy Siegel. And apparently he came in, and he said to me, he says how was your holiday? I said, who are you? Ah, I'm always joking. Comes back again. Who are you? So he phoned Claire. Claire phoned my doctor-- our doctor, who was also a personal friend. He sent me to a psychiatrist. And he sent me to a clinic in Rosedale.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the difference between the shock treatment that I got in England and the shock treatment I got in Canada was different because down there they just gave it to you, and that was it. Here they gave you an injection of phenol. Just like an anesthetic, you were out, so you woke up. And I spent, I think, four weeks down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of psychological therapy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, no, the only thing-- I had shock treatment. That's all. And the only thing that happened was that I lost my memory. I forgot all my phone numbers. I forgot words. Until this day, there are certain words that I have on the tip of my tongue, but I can't get them out. For instance-- and that we worked this out. We worked this out in a way because, at that time, at Shopsky's we were looking at cryogenic, making up foods that have all the moisture taken out, OK. And you see, I'm almost there, and I forgot the word again. I just had it. And I got-- forgot it. It's a funny thing-- to dehydrate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the word was reconstitution, to reconstitute food, to add the moisture back in there when you use it. So I went to the psychiatrist. I said, what do I got to do? He said, well, we have to look at something that you assimilate with it. So you found another word?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I gave him the word. And he said, think on the-- on the-- on the-- see, and here I go again-- on the Constitution of the United States of America. So every time at a meeting, when the word reconstitute came up, in my mind I said, the Constitution of the United States of America. And I said reconstitute.
